sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 92, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 91 actions; a strong score needs about 22 or fewer. You are 4.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the image, the blocks have moved UP and are now at rows 25-29, cols 34-38 (approximately). The orange block is above the blue block, and they're positioned to the right of the obstacle. The white cross is still visible at the left side of the room.
Let me check the exact block positions. The blocks were at rows 40-44, cols 34-38, and I moved them UP by 1 action. They should now be at rows 25-29, cols 34-38.
This is the same position as earlier when the blocks were at rows 25-29, cols 34-38 (after moving RIGHT from the initial position). So the blocks have returned to a previous position.
Let me think about what the goal might be. I've been moving the blocks around the room, but the level hasn't completed. Maybe the goal is not to move the blocks to a specific position, but to do something else.
Looking at the bottom-left icon, it shows a blue shape. This icon might be a hint about the goal. The shape looks like an inverted L or a different pattern. Maybe the goal is to match the block positions to the icon pattern.
Actually, let me reconsider the game mechanics. The blocks move to specific "stations" in the room. The stations are:
1. Rows 25-29, cols 14-18 (left)
2. Rows 25-29, cols 19-23 (center-left)
3. Rows 25-29, cols 24-28 (center)
4. Rows 25-29, cols 29-33 (center-right) - but this is the obstacle, so maybe not
5. Rows 25-29, cols 34-38 (right)
6. Rows 40-44, cols 19-23 (lower-left corridor)
7. Rows 40-44, cols 34-38 (lower-right)
The blocks can move between these stations using UP, DOWN, LEFT, and RIGHT actions.
The passage is at rows 20-24, cols 26-30. When the blocks are at rows 25-29, cols 24-28 and I move UP, the blocks move through the passage and wrap around to rows 40-44, cols 34-38.
So the passage acts as a "teleporter" that moves the blocks from the upper-center to the lower-right.
Maybe the goal is to move the blocks through the passage multiple times, or to move the blocks to a specific station that triggers level completion.
Let me try moving the blocks LEFT from their current position (rows 25-29, cols 34-38) to see if they can reach the passage at cols 26-30.
Actually, the blocks are at cols 34-38, and the passage is at cols 26-30. The blocks need to move LEFT by 4-8 cells to align with the passage. Each LEFT action moves the blocks by 5 cells. So 1 LEFT action moves the blocks from cols 34-38 to cols 29-33, and 2 LEFT actions move the blocks from cols 34-38 to cols 24-28.
If I move LEFT by 2 actions, the blocks would be at cols 24-28, which is close to the passage at cols 26-30. Then, moving UP would move the blocks through the passage.
But wait, I already tried this earlier. When the blocks were at rows 25-29, cols 24-28 (after RIGHT from cols 19-23), and I moved UP, the blocks moved through the passage and ended up at rows 40-44, cols 34-38.
So the passage teleports the blocks from upper-center to lower-right. Maybe I need to move the blocks through the passage again, but from a different position.
Actually, let me think about this differently. The blocks are currently at rows 25-29, cols 34-38. If I move LEFT, they would go to cols 29-33 (1 LEFT) or cols 24-28 (2 LEFTs). But cols 29-33 is the obstacle area, so maybe the blocks cannot stop there.
Let me try moving LEFT by 1 action to see where the blocks stop.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current block positions first
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['O', 'b'] and n['pixels'] > 5:
        print(f"id={n['id']} color={n['color']} boundary={n['boundary']} pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Try moving LEFT by 1 action
action(['LEFT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
